name: भक्तियोग
teaching: Between the formless absolute and the beloved with a face, Krishna gently favours the face — not because the formless is false, but because the embodied heart needs somewhere to rest. And if love is hard, practice; if practice is hard, work as offering; if even that is hard, simply let go of results. The chapter ends not with metaphysics but with a portrait of the devotee: friendly, compassionate, free of malice, the same in honour and disgrace. Between the formless and the beloved face, the Gita gently chooses the face — for embodied hearts love best what they can hold. A ladder is lowered to every capacity, and the chapter ends in the portrait the Lord calls dearest.
questions: Which rung of Krishna's ladder — absorption, practice, offered work, surrendered results — is actually within your reach this week?
Of the devotee's qualities listed at the end, which one is hardest for you?
Whom do you find easy to love — and is your spiritual life connected to that ease, or separate from it?

verses.speaker: अर्जुन उवाच
verses.devanagari:
एवं सततयुक्ता ये भक्तास्त्वां पर्युपासते ।
ये चाप्यक्षरमव्यक्तं तेषां के योगवित्तमाः॥
verses.transliteration:
evaṃ satatayuktā ye bhaktāstvāṃ paryupāsate
ye cāpyakṣaramavyaktaṃ teṣāṃ ke yogavittamāḥ
verses.essence: Arjuna asks: the devotees who worship you thus, ever-disciplined — and those who worship the Imperishable, the Unmanifest — which of these knows yoga better?
verses.translation: Those devotees who, ever steadfast, thus worship Thee and those also who worship the imperishable and the unmanifested which of them are better versed in Yoga?

anvaya.meaning: those thus constantly disciplined
the devotees who worship you
and those too who (worship) the Imperishable, Unmanifest
of these, which are the best knowers of yoga

verses.explanation: The question the vision made urgent: should one love the Person just seen, or contemplate the formless Absolute behind him? Arjuna asks like an engineer — which path is *better* — and his 'evam', 'thus', points back to 11.55's portrait. The whole chapter is the answer to this one fork.
verses.connection: Born directly of ch11: form and formless both revealed — now which to worship?
verses.reflection: Hold your own version of the fork honestly today: does your heart pray to a face, or a vastness?
verses.background: Arjuna, having seen both the cosmic blaze and the beloved face, asks the question the vision forced: of those who worship You with steadfast love, and those who seek the imperishable unmanifest — which are the better knowers of yoga?

verses.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
मय्यावेश्य मनो ये मां नित्ययुक्ता उपासते ।
श्रद्धया परयोपेताः ते मे युक्ततमा मताः॥
verses.transliteration:
mayyāveśya mano ye māṃ nityayuktā upāsate
śraddhayā parayopetāḥ te me yuktatamā matāḥ
verses.essence: The Blessed Lord says: those who, fixing their mind on me, worship me ever-disciplined and endowed with supreme faith — them I hold to be the most yoked.
verses.translation: Those who, fixing their mind on Me, worship Me, ever steadfast and endowed with supreme faith, are the best in Yoga in My opinion.

anvaya.meaning: fixing the mind in me, those who me
ever-disciplined, worship
endowed with supreme faith
they, in my view, are the most yoked

verses.explanation: A direct answer, rare in scripture: the form-lovers win — yuktatamāḥ, the same superlative that crowned 6.47. The deciding factors are named: āveśya, the mind not merely directed at him but *immersed*; and śraddhā parā, the highest grade of trust. Concreteness, it turns out, is not a concession but an advantage: love needs a face to find its full strength.
verses.connection: Answers 12.1 cleanly — then immediately honors the other path in 12.3–4.
verses.reflection: Let love have a face today — fix the mind on the concrete sacred and feel devotion gain traction.
verses.background: Krishna answers with a tenderness the tradition treasures: both reach Me, but those whose minds are fixed on Me with supreme faith I deem the most yoked — for the unmanifest path is steep for embodied beings, while those who offer all to Me and meditate on Me with undivided yoga, I swiftly lift from the ocean of death and rebirth.

verses.devanagari:
ये त्वक्षरमनिर्देश्यमव्यक्तं पर्युपासते ।
सर्वत्रगमचिन्त्यञ्च कूटस्थमचलन्ध्रुवम्॥
verses.transliteration:
ye tvakṣaramanirdeśyamavyaktaṃ paryupāsate
sarvatragamacintyañca kūṭasthamacalandhruvam
verses.essence: But those who worship the Imperishable — the undefinable, unmanifest, all-pervading, unthinkable, summit-still, immovable, constant —
verses.translation: Swami Sivananda did not comment on this sloka

anvaya.meaning: but those who (worship) the Imperishable, the indefinable
the Unmanifest — worship
the all-pervading, and the unthinkable
the summit-seated, unmoving, constant

verses.explanation: The other path described in its own austere vocabulary — seven negations and abstractions in two lines, each word a door closing on imagery: undefinable, unmanifest, unthinkable. To worship *this* is to hold the mind to what gives the mind nothing to hold. The sentence itself withholds its verb until the next verse — even the grammar makes you wait.
verses.connection: Begins the honest account of the formless path before its difficulty is weighed in 12.5.
verses.reflection: Spend two minutes with the attributeless — no image, no name — and honor those who dwell there.
verses.background: Krishna answers with a tenderness the tradition treasures: both reach Me, but those whose minds are fixed on Me with supreme faith I deem the most yoked — for the unmanifest path is steep for embodied beings, while those who offer all to Me and meditate on Me with undivided yoga, I swiftly lift from the ocean of death and rebirth.

verses.devanagari:
सन्नियम्येन्द्रियग्रामं सर्वत्र समबुद्धयः ।
ते प्राप्नुवन्ति मामेव सर्वभूतहिते रताः॥
verses.transliteration:
sanniyamyendriyagrāmaṃ sarvatra samabuddhayaḥ
te prāpnuvanti māmeva sarvabhūtahite ratāḥ
verses.essence: — restraining the host of senses, even-minded everywhere, delighting in the welfare of all beings: they also reach none but me.
verses.translation: Having restrained all the senses, even-minded everywhere, intent on the welfare of all beings verily they also come unto Me.

anvaya.meaning: fully restraining the troop of senses
everywhere of even understanding
they attain none other than me
delighting in the welfare of all beings

verses.explanation: The formless path completed, with its signature ethics: total sense-governance, evenness everywhere, and — the warmth inside the austerity — sarva-bhūta-hite ratāḥ, joy in every creature's good. And the destination clause settles all rivalry: mām eva, *me alone*. There were never two summits; the impersonal seekers arrive at the same person by another face.
verses.connection: Closes the question of destination — both roads end in him — leaving only the question of cost.
verses.reflection: Whichever path you walk, keep its ethic today: senses governed, delight in every being's welfare.
verses.background: Krishna answers with a tenderness the tradition treasures: both reach Me, but those whose minds are fixed on Me with supreme faith I deem the most yoked — for the unmanifest path is steep for embodied beings, while those who offer all to Me and meditate on Me with undivided yoga, I swiftly lift from the ocean of death and rebirth.

verses.devanagari:
क्लेशोऽधिकतरस्तेषामव्यक्तासक्तचेतसाम् ।
अव्यक्ता हि गतिर्दुःखं देहवद्भिरवाप्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
kleśo'dhikatarasteṣāmavyaktāsaktacetasām ||
avyaktā hi gatirduḥkhaṃ dehavadbhiravāpyate
verses.essence: Greater is the toil of those whose minds are set on the Unmanifest, for the unmanifest goal is reached by the embodied only with difficulty.
verses.translation: Greater is their trouble whose minds are set on the unmanifested; for the goal; the unmanifested, is very hard for the embodied to reach.

anvaya.meaning: greater is the affliction of those
whose minds are attached to the Unmanifest
for the unmanifest goal, with difficulty
is attained by the embodied

verses.explanation: The cost accounting: kleśo 'dhikataraḥ — more friction, not less truth. The reason is in one word: dehavadbhiḥ, *by those who have bodies*. Embodied beings love through eyes, hands, voice; asking such creatures to adore a featureless absolute is asking a river to flow without banks. The formless path is honored — and priced for what it asks of incarnate hearts.
verses.connection: The practical verdict behind 12.2's ranking: same summit, steeper face.
verses.schools_note: Schools divide on the force of this verse: Advaita reads the klesha as provisional — the formless path is harder but ultimate; Vaishnava commentators read it as the Gita's standing recommendation of the personal God for embodied souls. The text itself states difficulty, not inferiority.
verses.reflection: Be kind to your embodiment today — you love through eyes and hands; let your practice use them.
verses.background: Krishna answers with a tenderness the tradition treasures: both reach Me, but those whose minds are fixed on Me with supreme faith I deem the most yoked — for the unmanifest path is steep for embodied beings, while those who offer all to Me and meditate on Me with undivided yoga, I swiftly lift from the ocean of death and rebirth.

verses.devanagari:
ये तु सर्वाणि कर्माणि मयि संन्यस्य मत्परः ।
अनन्येनैव योगेन मां ध्यायन्त उपासते॥
verses.transliteration:
ye tu sarvāṇi karmāṇi mayi saṃnyasya matparaḥ
ananyenaiva yogena māṃ dhyāyanta upāsate
verses.essence: But those who, renouncing all actions in me and holding me supreme, worship me, meditating with a yoga turned to no other —
verses.translation: But to those who worship Me, renouncing all actions in Me, regarding Me as the supreme gaol, meditating on Me with single-minded Yoga.

anvaya.meaning: but those who all actions
renouncing in me, holding me supreme
with a yoga directed to none other
worship me, meditating

verses.explanation: The recommended path drawn in full: every action deposited in him (the trustee-relation of 3.30 returning), him as the one direction, meditation that does not glance sideways. Ananyena — the chapter's recurring key — no second object, no hedge, no backup deity. The sentence leans forward into the promise of the next verse.
verses.connection: Describes the devotee whose rescue 12.7 will announce.
verses.reflection: Deposit every action in Him today as you act — sannyasya mayi — and meditate on no second thing.
verses.background: Krishna answers with a tenderness the tradition treasures: both reach Me, but those whose minds are fixed on Me with supreme faith I deem the most yoked — for the unmanifest path is steep for embodied beings, while those who offer all to Me and meditate on Me with undivided yoga, I swiftly lift from the ocean of death and rebirth.

verses.devanagari:
तेषामहं समुद्धर्ता मृत्युसंसारसागरात् ।
भवामि नचिरात्पार्थ मय्यावेशितचेतसाम्॥
verses.transliteration:
teṣāmahaṃ samuddhartā mṛtyusaṃsārasāgarāt
bhavāmi nacirātpārtha mayyāveśitacetasām
verses.essence: — for them I become, before long, the deliverer from the ocean of death-bound existence, Partha, their minds absorbed in me.
verses.translation: To those whose minds are set on Me, O Arjuna, verily I become ere long the saviour out of the ocean of Samsara.

anvaya.meaning: of them I am the uplifter
from the ocean of death-and-rebirth
I become, before long, O Partha
whose minds are absorbed in me

verses.explanation: The chapter's central transaction, and the grammar carries the theology: ahaṁ samuddhartā — *I* am the lifter-out. On the formless path the seeker swims the ocean; on this one, the Ocean's master reaches down. Na cirāt, without delay: grace does not keep office hours. The devotee's one task was completed in the previous verse — being findable, mind already in him.
verses.connection: The payoff of 12.6's surrender — and the deepest reason 12.2 ranked this path first.
verses.reflection: When the ocean feels wide today, remember who lifts the surrendered — and stop swimming alone.
verses.background: Krishna answers with a tenderness the tradition treasures: both reach Me, but those whose minds are fixed on Me with supreme faith I deem the most yoked — for the unmanifest path is steep for embodied beings, while those who offer all to Me and meditate on Me with undivided yoga, I swiftly lift from the ocean of death and rebirth.

verses.devanagari:
मय्येव मन आधत्स्व मयि बुद्धिं निवेशय ।
निवसिष्यसि मय्येव अत ऊर्ध्वं न संशयः॥
verses.transliteration:
mayyeva mana ādhatsva mayi buddhiṃ niveśaya
nivasiṣyasi mayyeva ata ūrdhvaṃ na saṃśayaḥ
verses.essence: Place your mind in me alone; let your understanding enter into me. You will dwell in me thereafter — of this there is no doubt.
verses.translation: Fix thy mind in Me only, thy intellect in Me, (then) thou shalt no doubt live in Me alone hereafter.

anvaya.meaning: in me alone place the mind
in me settle the understanding
you shall dwell in me alone
hereafter — there is no doubt

verses.explanation: The instruction at its purest, before any concessions: both faculties — manas, the feeling-flowing mind, and buddhi, the deciding intellect — given one address. The promise uses the language of residence: nivasiṣyasi, you will *live* in me, not visit. And the chapter's gentlest reassurance closes it: na saṁśayaḥ, spoken to the man whose doubts have filled eleven chapters. Mayy eva mana adhatsva — place the mind in Me alone, settle the intellect in Me: the ladder's top rung stated first, and the commentaries note both faculties named — manas, the feeling current, and buddhi, the deciding one. Nivasishyasi mayy eva — you will dwell in Me: the verb of residence, not visitation. Ata urdhvam na samshayah: the chapter's signature reassurance, addressed to a doubter.
verses.connection: Step one of the famous ladder (12.8–11): the ideal, stated first so the descents have a summit.
verses.reflection: Try the ideal first, tonight: mind placed in Him, intellect entered into Him — and dwell, doubtless.
verses.background: A ladder is lowered rung by rung to every capacity: fix mind and intellect in Me and dwell in Me — if you cannot, reach Me by repeated practice; if not that, work for My sake; if even that is beyond you, then do your duty and renounce the fruit — for the renunciation of fruit brings immediate peace.

verses.devanagari:
अथ चित्तं समाधातुं न शक्नोषि मयि स्थिरम् ।
अभ्यासयोगेन ततो मामिच्छाप्तुं धनञ्जय॥
verses.transliteration:
atha cittaṃ samādhātuṃ na śaknoṣi mayi sthiram
abhyāsayogena tato māmicchāptuṃ dhanañjaya
verses.essence: But if you cannot fix your mind steadily on me, then seek to reach me by the yoga of repeated practice, Dhananjaya.
verses.translation: If thou art unable to fix thy mind steadily on Me, then by the Yoga of constant practice do thou seek to reach Me, O Arjuna.

anvaya.meaning: now, if the mind to fix
you are not able, steadily, on me
then by the yoga of practice
seek to attain me, O Dhananjaya

verses.explanation: The first 'if you cannot' — three words that make this chapter the kindest in the Gita. Steady absorption unavailable? Then abhyāsa: the unsteady version, done again and again, the mind brought back each time it strays (6.26's old patience). The ladder's genius is that no rung shames the one below it; each is simply where someone real is standing.
verses.connection: Rung two: when 12.8's steadiness is out of reach, repetition is the road to it.
verses.reflection: Cannot hold steady? Then repeat: return the mind each time it strays — abhyasa is the unsteady's road.
verses.background: A ladder is lowered rung by rung to every capacity: fix mind and intellect in Me and dwell in Me — if you cannot, reach Me by repeated practice; if not that, work for My sake; if even that is beyond you, then do your duty and renounce the fruit — for the renunciation of fruit brings immediate peace.

verses.devanagari:
अभ्यासेऽप्यसमर्थोऽसि मत्कर्मपरमो भव ।
मदर्थमपि कर्माणि कुर्वन्सिद्धिमवाप्स्यसि॥
verses.transliteration:
abhyāse'pyasamartho'si matkarmaparamo bhava
madarthamapi karmāṇi kurvansiddhimavāpsyasi
verses.essence: If you are incapable even of practice, be intent on working for me; even doing actions for my sake, you will attain perfection.
verses.translation: If thou art unable to practise even this Abhyasa Yoga, be thou intent on doing actions for My sake; even by doing actions for My sake, thou shalt attain perfection.

anvaya.meaning: if even in practice you are unable
become devoted to work for me
actions even for my sake
performing, you will attain perfection

verses.explanation: Rung three descends from the mind to the hands: cannot sit and practice? Then *do* — but do for him. Mad-artham, for my sake: the same workday, re-addressed, becomes worship; cooking, building, fighting, healing — each act a letter posted to one recipient. Perfection, the verse insists, is reachable through the calendar as surely as through the cushion.
verses.connection: Rung three: contemplation replaced by consecrated action — the karma-yoga of Part One, now love-shaped.
verses.reflection: Cannot even practice? Re-address one task today: done for His sake — and let work become the worship.
verses.background: A ladder is lowered rung by rung to every capacity: fix mind and intellect in Me and dwell in Me — if you cannot, reach Me by repeated practice; if not that, work for My sake; if even that is beyond you, then do your duty and renounce the fruit — for the renunciation of fruit brings immediate peace.

verses.devanagari:
अथैतदप्यशक्तोऽसि कर्तुं मद्योगमाश्रितः ।
सर्वकर्मफलत्यागं ततः कुरु यतात्मवान्॥
verses.transliteration:
athaitadapyaśakto'si kartuṃ madyogamāśritaḥ
sarvakarmaphalatyāgaṃ tataḥ kuru yatātmavān
verses.essence: If you cannot do even this, then, taking refuge in my yoga, renounce the fruit of all action with the self subdued.
verses.translation: If thou art unable to do even this, then, resorting to union with Me, renounce the fruits of all actions with the self controlled.

anvaya.meaning: now, if even this you are unable
to do — taking refuge in my yoga
the renunciation of the fruit of all actions
then perform, with self restrained

verses.explanation: The lowest rung, and the most universally reachable: cannot even dedicate the work? Then keep working as you do — only let go of the outcome. No God-language required, no altered schedule; just the open hand where the clutch used to be. The ladder bottoms out at something *anyone*, of any belief, can begin this afternoon. No one is left without a first step.
verses.connection: The final concession — which 12.12 will reveal is no concession at all.
verses.reflection: Cannot manage even that? Then do today's duties and release the results — the lowest rung lifts too.
verses.background: A ladder is lowered rung by rung to every capacity: fix mind and intellect in Me and dwell in Me — if you cannot, reach Me by repeated practice; if not that, work for My sake; if even that is beyond you, then do your duty and renounce the fruit — for the renunciation of fruit brings immediate peace.

verses.devanagari:
श्रेयो हि ज्ञानमभ्यासाज्ज्ञानाद्ध्यानं विशिष्यते ।
ध्यानात्कर्मफलत्यागस्त्यागाच्छान्तिरनन्तरम्॥
verses.transliteration:
śreyo hi jñānamabhyāsājjñānāddhyānaṃ viśiṣyate
dhyānātkarmaphalatyāgastyāgācchāntiranantaram
verses.essence: Better than practice is knowledge; better than knowledge, meditation; better than meditation, the renunciation of action's fruit — from renunciation, peace immediately.
verses.translation: Better indeed is knowledge than practice; than knowledge meditation is better; than meditation the renunciation of the fruits of actions: peace immediately follows renunciation.

anvaya.meaning: better indeed is knowledge than practice
than knowledge, meditation excels
than meditation, renunciation of action's fruit
from renunciation — peace, immediately

verses.explanation: The chapter's sly reversal: the rung offered last as a concession is here ranked *first* in effect. Why? Because rote practice, book-knowledge, even meditation can all be performed by an ego still gripping its outcomes — while phala-tyāga is the grip itself released. And the timing claim is the boldest in the Gita: śāntir anantaram — peace not after lifetimes, but immediately. The hand opens; the war ends. Shreyo hi jnanam abhyasat — the ranking verse that puzzles on first reading: knowledge above practice, meditation above knowledge, and above meditation — the renunciation of fruit, with peace immediately after. The commentaries resolve it variously, but most read the point practically: the humblest-looking step is the most direct, because phala-tyaga is the one move the ego cannot co-opt. Shantir anantaram: the Gita's shortest delivery time.
verses.connection: Re-values the ladder of 12.8–11: the humblest rung touches the goal most directly — and opens the portrait that follows.
verses.schools_note: The ascending ranking that ends with fruit-renunciation above meditation puzzled every commentator. Shankara resolves it as ranking for the one unable to do the prior practice (a descent restated as ascent); Ramanuja reads it as praise of karma-phala-tyaga as the safest universal means; Madhusudana as rhetorical climax. Practitioners across schools converge on its lived sense: the simplest release yields the most immediate peace.
verses.reflection: Take the shortcut hidden at the bottom today: renounce one outcome and collect the immediate peace.
verses.background: A ladder is lowered rung by rung to every capacity: fix mind and intellect in Me and dwell in Me — if you cannot, reach Me by repeated practice; if not that, work for My sake; if even that is beyond you, then do your duty and renounce the fruit — for the renunciation of fruit brings immediate peace.

verses.devanagari:
अद्वेष्टा सर्वभूतानां मैत्रः करुण एव च ।
निर्ममो निरहङ्कारः समदुःखसुखः क्षमी॥
verses.transliteration:
adveṣṭā sarvabhūtānāṃ maitraḥ karuṇa eva ca
nirmamo nirahaṅkāraḥ samaduḥkhasukhaḥ kṣamī
verses.essence: Without hatred toward any being, friendly and compassionate, free of 'mine' and free of 'I', equal in sorrow and joy, forbearing —
verses.translation: He who hates no creature, who is friendly and compassionate to all, who is free from attachment and egoism, balanced in pleasure and pain, and forgiving.

anvaya.meaning: non-hating toward all beings
friendly, and compassionate
without mine-ness, without ego
equal in sorrow and joy, forbearing

verses.explanation: The beloved-devotee portrait begins (12.13–19) — the Gita's most cherished passage, recited daily across centuries. First the foundation, and it is social before it is mystical: adveṣṭā, hating no one — not even those who qualify. Then maitra, karuṇa: friendship and compassion as default settings. The saint is described entirely by how it feels to be near them. Adveshta sarva-bhutanam — the beloved-devotee portrait opens, the passage Gandhi recited every evening for decades and called his daily mirror. The first credential is negative — hating none — then maitrah karunah, friendly, compassionate. The tradition notes what is absent from the entire portrait: no austerities, no visions, no learning. The saint is described entirely as a way of being-with.
verses.connection: Opens the answer to an unasked question: what does 12.12's released person actually look like?
verses.reflection: Run the foundation check today: hating no being, friendly, compassionate — begin the portrait there.
verses.background: The chapter closes with the portrait Gandhi recited nightly for decades — the devotee dear to Krishna: hating none, friendly and compassionate, free of 'I' and 'mine', even in pleasure and pain, content, self-governed, agitating no one and unagitated, the same in honor and disgrace — and the seal: those who follow this immortal dharma with faith, holding Me supreme, are exceedingly dear to Me.

verses.devanagari:
सन्तुष्टः सततं योगी यतात्मा दृढनिश्चयः ।
मय्यर्पितमनोबुद्धिर्यो मद्भक्तः स मे प्रियः॥
verses.transliteration:
santuṣṭaḥ satataṃ yogī yatātmā dṛḍhaniścayaḥ
mayyarpitamanobuddhiryo madbhaktaḥ sa me priyaḥ
verses.essence: — ever content, a yogi, self-governed, of firm resolve, with mind and understanding offered to me: that devotee of mine is dear to me.
verses.translation: Ever content, steady in meditation, self-controlled, possessed of firm conviction, with the mind and intellect dedicated to Me, he, My devtoee, is dear to Me.

anvaya.meaning: ever content, a yogi
self-controlled, of firm conviction
with mind and understanding offered to me
who is my devotee — that one is dear to me

verses.explanation: Santuṣṭaḥ satatam — content *always*, contentment uncoupled from supply. Dṛḍha-niścayaḥ: gentleness, the portrait insists, is not vagueness; the soft-hearted devotee has a spine of resolve. And then the refrain enters that will toll through seven verses: sa me priyaḥ — that one is dear to me. God confessing, repeatedly, whom he loves.
verses.connection: First sounding of the chapter's refrain — the divine 'dear to me' that structures 12.14–19.
verses.reflection: Be content once today with what is, while staying firm about what you are doing — both at once.
verses.background: The chapter closes with the portrait Gandhi recited nightly for decades — the devotee dear to Krishna: hating none, friendly and compassionate, free of 'I' and 'mine', even in pleasure and pain, content, self-governed, agitating no one and unagitated, the same in honor and disgrace — and the seal: those who follow this immortal dharma with faith, holding Me supreme, are exceedingly dear to Me.

verses.devanagari:
यस्मान्नोद्विजते लोको लोकान्नोद्विजते च यः ।
हर्षामर्षभयोद्वेगैर्मुक्तो यः स च मे प्रियः॥
verses.transliteration:
yasmānnodvijate loko lokānnodvijate ca yaḥ
harṣāmarṣabhayodvegairmukto yaḥ sa ca me priyaḥ
verses.essence: One from whom the world does not shrink, and who does not shrink from the world; freed from elation, impatience, fear and agitation — that one is dear to me.
verses.translation: He by whom the world is not agitated and who cannot be agitated by the world, and who is freed from joy, anger, fear and anxiety he is dear to Me.

anvaya.meaning: from whom the world does not shrink
and who does not shrink from the world
from elation, impatience, fear, agitation

anvaya.word: मुक्तः यः सः च मे प्रियः
anvaya.meaning: who is freed — that one too is dear to me

verses.explanation: A two-way peace treaty with existence: na udvijate, twice — the world is not disturbed by them, they are not disturbed by the world. Such a person is, in the deepest sense, *safe* — safe to be around, safe inside themselves. The four named turbulences (thrill, irritation, fear, anxiety) are the weather they no longer generate or import. Yasman nodvijate loko — from whom the world does not shrink, and who does not shrink from the world: the double-direction peace the commentaries call the social test of realization. Harshamarsha-bhayodvegair muktah — free of the four turbulences. Jnaneshwar's gloss is loved: such a one is like a lake so still the village finally sees its own face in it.
verses.connection: The portrait's social test: the devotee as a still point the world can rest near.
verses.reflection: Aim at the double peace today: agitate no one, and let no one's agitation move into you.
verses.background: The chapter closes with the portrait Gandhi recited nightly for decades — the devotee dear to Krishna: hating none, friendly and compassionate, free of 'I' and 'mine', even in pleasure and pain, content, self-governed, agitating no one and unagitated, the same in honor and disgrace — and the seal: those who follow this immortal dharma with faith, holding Me supreme, are exceedingly dear to Me.

verses.devanagari:
अनपेक्षः शुचिर्दक्ष उदासीनो गतव्यथः ।
सर्वारम्भपरित्यागी यो मद्भक्तः स मे प्रियः॥
verses.transliteration:
anapekṣaḥ śucirdakṣa udāsīno gatavyathaḥ
sarvārambhaparityāgī yo madbhaktaḥ sa me priyaḥ
verses.essence: Expecting nothing, pure, capable, impartial, untroubled, renouncing every self-serving undertaking — that devotee of mine is dear to me.
verses.translation: He who is free from wants, pure, expert, unconcerned, and free from pain, renouncing all undertakings or commencements he who is (thus) devoted to Me, is dear to Me.

anvaya.meaning: expectation-free, pure, skillful
impartial, with anguish gone
renouncer of all (selfish) undertakings
who is my devotee — that one is dear to me

verses.explanation: Note dakṣaḥ — *skillful*, expert: the beloved devotee is not vague or otherworldly but competent, sharp at their work; holiness here includes craftsmanship. Anapekṣaḥ, expecting nothing: the quiet engine of all the rest. Sarvārambha-parityāgī: not the end of activity, but of the scheming kind — projects launched to inflate a self.
verses.connection: Corrects the stereotype mid-portrait: detachment wedded to excellence.
verses.reflection: Pair purity with skill today — be the devotee who is also simply excellent at the work.
verses.background: The chapter closes with the portrait Gandhi recited nightly for decades — the devotee dear to Krishna: hating none, friendly and compassionate, free of 'I' and 'mine', even in pleasure and pain, content, self-governed, agitating no one and unagitated, the same in honor and disgrace — and the seal: those who follow this immortal dharma with faith, holding Me supreme, are exceedingly dear to Me.

verses.devanagari:
यो न हृष्यति न द्वेष्टि न शोचति न काङ्क्षति ।
शुभाशुभपरित्यागी भक्तिमान्यः स मे प्रियः॥
verses.transliteration:
yo na hṛṣyati na dveṣṭi na śocati na kāṅkṣati
śubhāśubhaparityāgī bhaktimānyaḥ sa me priyaḥ
verses.essence: One who neither rejoices nor hates, neither grieves nor craves, who has renounced both the pleasant and the unpleasant — full of devotion, that one is dear to me.
verses.translation: He who neither rejoices, nor hates, nor grieves, nor desires, renouncing good and evil, and who is full of devotion, is dear to Me.

anvaya.meaning: who neither exults nor hates
neither grieves nor craves
renouncer of the pleasant and unpleasant
who is full of devotion — that one is dear to me

verses.explanation: Four verbs of the heart's old weather — exulting, hating, mourning, wanting — all quieted; even the moral seesaw of śubha and aśubha set down. What remains when all that is renounced? The verse answers in one word, placed like a signature: bhaktimān — *full of love*. The emptying was never toward emptiness; everything else left so that devotion could have the whole house.
verses.connection: The portrait's secret revealed: every renunciation listed was making room for love.
verses.reflection: Make room for love today by quieting one of the four: exulting, hating, grieving, craving.
verses.background: The chapter closes with the portrait Gandhi recited nightly for decades — the devotee dear to Krishna: hating none, friendly and compassionate, free of 'I' and 'mine', even in pleasure and pain, content, self-governed, agitating no one and unagitated, the same in honor and disgrace — and the seal: those who follow this immortal dharma with faith, holding Me supreme, are exceedingly dear to Me.

verses.devanagari:
समः शत्रौ च मित्रे च तथा मानापमानयोः ।
शीतोष्णसुखदुःखेषु समः सङ्गविवर्जितः॥
verses.transliteration:
samaḥ śatrau ca mitre ca tathā mānāpamānayoḥ
śītoṣṇasukhaduḥkheṣu samaḥ saṅgavivarjitaḥ
verses.essence: Equal toward enemy and friend, and in honor and disgrace; equal in cold and heat, joy and sorrow; free of attachment —
verses.translation: Swami Sivananda did not comment on this sloka

anvaya.meaning: equal toward enemy and friend
likewise in honor and dishonor
in cold-heat, joy-sorrow
equal, free from attachment

verses.explanation: The equality ladder climbs its full height: weather (cold-heat), feeling (joy-sorrow), reputation (honor-disgrace), and at the top the steepest rung — śatrau ca mitre ca, enemy and friend met with the same face. For Arjuna, looking across a field at men who are precisely both, the phrase is not abstract ethics; it is his Tuesday.
verses.connection: The evenness of chapters 2–6 returns — now as a lover's trait rather than a technique.
verses.reflection: Try one hour of the steep equality: the same inner face toward a friend and toward a difficult one.
verses.background: The chapter closes with the portrait Gandhi recited nightly for decades — the devotee dear to Krishna: hating none, friendly and compassionate, free of 'I' and 'mine', even in pleasure and pain, content, self-governed, agitating no one and unagitated, the same in honor and disgrace — and the seal: those who follow this immortal dharma with faith, holding Me supreme, are exceedingly dear to Me.

verses.devanagari:
तुल्यनिन्दास्तुतिर्मौनी सन्तुष्टो येन केनचित् ।
अनिकेतः स्थिरमतिर्भक्तिमान्मे प्रियो नरः॥
verses.transliteration:
tulyanindāstutirmaunī santuṣṭo yena kenacit
aniketaḥ sthiramatirbhaktimānme priyo naraḥ
verses.essence: — alike in blame and praise, silent, content with whatever comes, homeless, steady-minded, full of devotion: that one is dear to me.
verses.translation: He to whom censure and praise are eal, who is silent, content with anything, homeless, of a steady mind, and full of devotion that man is dear to Me.

anvaya.meaning: alike in blame and praise, silent
content with whatever (comes)
without a home, of steady mind
full of devotion — dear to me is that person

verses.explanation: The portrait's last strokes: tulya-nindā-stutiḥ — the two voices that rule most lives, criticism and applause, finally at the same volume; maunī, the silence of one with nothing to defend; aniketaḥ, homeless — read literally by renunciants, inwardly by householders: at home everywhere because rooted nowhere circumstantial. And once more, bhaktimān: love, the trait the list cannot stop repeating.
verses.connection: Completes the seven-verse portrait — before 12.20 turns it into an invitation.
verses.reflection: Turn praise and blame to the same volume for one day — and find home wherever you are standing.
verses.background: The chapter closes with the portrait Gandhi recited nightly for decades — the devotee dear to Krishna: hating none, friendly and compassionate, free of 'I' and 'mine', even in pleasure and pain, content, self-governed, agitating no one and unagitated, the same in honor and disgrace — and the seal: those who follow this immortal dharma with faith, holding Me supreme, are exceedingly dear to Me.

verses.devanagari:
ये तु धर्म्यामृतमिदं यथोक्तं पर्युपासते ।
श्रद्दधाना मत्परमा भक्तास्तेऽतीव मे प्रियाः॥
verses.transliteration:
ye tu dharmyāmṛtamidaṃ yathoktaṃ paryupāsate
śraddadhānā matparamā bhaktāste'tīva me priyāḥ
verses.essence: But those who honor this nectar of dharma as it has been declared, full of faith, holding me as supreme — those devotees are exceedingly dear to me.
verses.translation: They verily who follow this immortal Dharma (law or doctrine) as described above, endowed with faith, regarding Me as their supreme goal, they, the devotees, are exceedingly dear to Me.

anvaya.meaning: but those who this nectar of dharma
as declared, devotedly follow
full of faith, holding me supreme
those devotees are exceedingly dear to me

verses.explanation: The closing widens the door: the superlative — atīva me priyāḥ, *exceedingly* dear — goes not to those who have completed the portrait but to those who paryupāsate, who keep honoring it, walking toward it with faith. The standard of 12.13–19 might discourage; this verse insures against that: the attempt itself, sincerely sustained, already receives the fullest love. The chapter ends, fittingly, mid-embrace.
verses.connection: Seals the bhakti chapter: the portrait was the destination; faith-filled practice of it is already arrival.
verses.reflection: You need not finish the portrait to be loved — follow it with faith today, and be already exceedingly dear.
verses.background: The chapter closes with the portrait Gandhi recited nightly for decades — the devotee dear to Krishna: hating none, friendly and compassionate, free of 'I' and 'mine', even in pleasure and pain, content, self-governed, agitating no one and unagitated, the same in honor and disgrace — and the seal: those who follow this immortal dharma with faith, holding Me supreme, are exceedingly dear to Me.
